name: First Paragraph Answers Query
description: Verifies that the first 150 words contain a declarative entity definition (entity name + category + primary function). Self-contained answer blocks near the top of the page are the primary extraction target for AI answer engines.
method_detail: First 150 words must contain: entity name + category + function in "X is a Y that does Z" format

criteria.pass: First 150 words contain ALL THREE: (a) entity/brand name, (b) category/type word, (c) primary function — in declarative "X is a Y that does Z" format
criteria.warn: Entity name present but category or function definition missing
criteria.fail: First 150 words contain no declarative entity definition (opens with tagline, story, or preamble)

fix_template: "[Entity] is a [category] [for audience] that [primary function]. [Key differentiator]. [Proof point or quantitative claim]."
sources: AI Overviews: self-contained 134-167 word blocks score 4.2x higher citation. Perplexity L3 reranker scores entity clarity. GEO paper: fluency + clarity = 15-30% visibility boost.

name: Quick-Answer Block Near H1
description: Checks for a summary, callout, or TL;DR block within 200 words of the H1 that directly answers the primary query. AI extractors scan top-of-page content first, and a delineated answer block signals "this is the answer".
method_detail: Check for summary/callout within 200 words of H1

criteria.pass: Within 200 words of H1, there is a summary, callout, or TL;DR block that directly answers the primary query
criteria.warn: Answer exists but not within first 200 words after H1
criteria.fail: No quick-answer element anywhere near the top of content

fix_template:
Add a summary block immediately after H1: `
[2-3 sentence direct answer to the primary query]
`
sources: AI extractors scan top-of-page content first. A clearly delineated answer block (styled differently, in a callout/box) signals "this is the answer" to both AI and users.

name: FAQ Section With 3+ Q&A Pairs
description: Verifies the page has an identifiable FAQ section with at least 3 question-answer pairs. FAQ content is the most directly extractable format for AI answer engines because questions map directly to user queries.

criteria.pass: Page has identifiable FAQ section with 3+ question-answer pairs

fix_template: Add FAQ section with 5-8 Q&A pairs. Derive questions from target queries + "People Also Ask" patterns.
sources: FAQPage schema = 3.2x citation increase. FAQ content is the most directly extractable format for AI answer engines. Questions map directly to user queries.

name: FAQ Uses Semantic Markup
description: Checks that FAQ content uses semantic HTML (details/summary, dl/dt/dd, or clearly structured Q/A markup) so AI parsers can identify question and answer boundaries.
method_detail: Check details/summary or clear Q+A structure

criteria.pass: FAQ uses `<details>/<summary>`, `<dl>/<dt>/<dd>`, or clearly structured Q/A HTML
criteria.warn: FAQ exists but uses generic divs without semantic structure
criteria.fail: FAQ content with no semantic markup or accessibility

sources: Semantic markup helps AI parsers identify question boundaries and answer boundaries. `<details>/<summary>` is native HTML — no JS needed.

name: FAQ Questions Use Natural Language
description: Verifies that FAQ questions are phrased as natural-language questions (How/What/When/Why/Is/Can/Does/Where/Which) rather than statements or branded phrases, so they match how users actually query AI engines.

criteria.pass: 80%+ of FAQ questions start with How/What/When/Why/Is/Can/Does/Where/Which

fix_template: Rephrase FAQ headings as natural questions: "Features" → "What features does [brand] offer?"
sources: AI query matching works best with natural language questions. "How does TRYPS work?" matches user queries better than "TRYPS Features" as an FAQ heading.

name: Headings Phrased as Questions or Answers
description: Checks whether H2/H3 headings are phrased as questions or direct answers instead of branded or abstract labels. AI systems match user queries against heading text to locate relevant sections.
method_detail: H2/H3 text has question marks or direct-answer phrasing

criteria.pass: 50%+ of H2/H3 headings contain question marks OR are direct-answer phrases
criteria.fail: < 25% (most headings are branded or abstract labels)

fix_template: Convert "Our Process" → "How does [brand] work?" or "Simple Process" → "3 Steps to Plan Your Group Trip"
sources: Question-style H2s create natural query-answer mapping. AI systems match user queries against heading text to locate relevant sections.

name: Named Entities (Not Vague Pronouns)
description: Verifies that specific entity names outnumber vague pronouns in the first 300 words. AI extractors need entity clarity to understand what the content is about — "[Brand] helps [audience]" is extractable, "our platform helps your team" is not.
method_detail: Count entity names vs "it","they","our","your" in first 300 words. Ratio > 3:1 = pass

criteria.pass: In first 300 words, specific entity names outnumber vague pronouns ("it", "they", "we", "our", "your", "this") by 3:1 ratio or better

fix_template: Replace vague references with specific names. "We help you" → "[Brand] helps [audience]". "Our tool" → "[Product name]".
sources: AI extractors need entity clarity to understand WHAT the content is about. "Our platform helps your team" is ambiguous. "[Brand] helps [specific audience]" is extractable. Perplexity L3 scores entity clarity.

name: Specific Facts (Numbers, Dates, Prices)
description: Counts quantitative claims (numbers, percentages, dates, prices, durations) in the content. AI systems prefer citable facts over vague marketing claims.
method_detail: Regex count: quantities, dates, currency, percentages

criteria.pass: 5+ quantitative claims in content (numbers, percentages, dates, prices, durations)

fix_template: Replace vague claims with specific data. "Saves time" → "Saves an average of 3 hours per trip." "Many users" → "Used by 500+ groups."
sources: GEO paper: "Statistics Addition" = 30-40% visibility boost for law/government, strong for all domains. AI systems prefer citable facts over vague claims. Sieve Anti-pattern #797: "Using Vague Marketing Language Instead of Verifiable Claims."

name: Definition-First Writing Style
description: Verifies the first sentence of the page is an entity definition ("X is a Y" pattern). Definition-first openings create an extractable entity description; AI Overview citation blocks start with entity definitions.
method_detail: First sentence matches "X is a Y" or "X is [article] Y that" pattern

criteria.pass: First sentence of the page matches "X is a Y" or "X is [article] Y that [verb]" pattern
criteria.warn: First sentence contains the entity name but in non-definitional format
criteria.fail: First sentence is a tagline, question, or narrative opener with no entity definition

fix_template: Start with: "[Entity] is a [category noun] that/for [function/audience]."
sources: Definition-first opening creates an extractable entity description. AI Overview citation blocks start with entity definitions. Perplexity L3 reranker scores entity clarity.

name: Summary/TL;DR at End
description: Checks that the last 20% of content contains a labeled summary section. End-of-article summaries provide a second extraction point for AI systems and bookend the opening answer block.
method_detail: Scan last 20% of content for summary section

criteria.pass: Last 20% of content contains a summary section (heading includes "summary", "takeaway", "conclusion", "TL;DR", "key points")
criteria.warn: Closing section exists but not clearly labeled as summary
criteria.fail: Content ends without any summary or recap

fix_template: Add section: `## Key Takeaways` with 3-5 bullet points summarizing the core information
sources: End-of-article summaries provide a second extraction point for AI systems. Users who scroll to the end get a recap. Creates bookend with the opening answer block.

name: Self-Contained Answer Units
description: Assesses whether each H2 section can be understood independently if extracted alone. AI engines extract sections, not full pages — self-contained sections are citation candidates, context-dependent ones are not.
method_detail: Each H2 section independently comprehensible if extracted alone

criteria.pass: Each H2 section can be understood independently if extracted alone — contains enough context to be a standalone answer
criteria.warn: Most sections are self-contained but 1-2 depend on prior sections for context
criteria.fail: Sections heavily reference prior content ("as mentioned above", "building on the previous point") and cannot stand alone

fix_template: Start each section with a brief contextual sentence that doesn't assume the reader saw previous sections
sources: AI engines extract SECTIONS, not full pages. Each section is a potential citation candidate. Self-contained = citable. Context-dependent = not citable.

name: Tables/Lists for Comparative Data
description: Verifies that multi-item or comparative data is presented in tables or structured lists rather than paragraph prose. Tables and lists are highly extractable by all AI engines.
method_detail: Count table + ol/ul elements in body

criteria.pass: Page uses `<table>` or structured `<ul>/<ol>` for presenting multi-item or comparative data
criteria.warn: Lists exist but data that should be in a table is in paragraph form
criteria.fail: No tables or structured lists, and content contains comparative or multi-item data

fix_template: Convert comparison paragraphs to `<table>` with clear column headers
sources: 40-61% of AI Overviews use list/bullet formats. Tables are highly extractable by all AI engines. Comparison tables map directly to "[Product] vs [Product]" queries.
